questions.stem: Biodiversity is defined as the variety of life in all its forms, levels and combinations. Which three types are included?
questions.explanation: Biodiversity includes ecosystem diversity, species diversity and genetic diversity.

questions.scenario: The variety of different genes within a single species is being described.
questions.stem: This is which type of biodiversity?
questions.explanation: Variation in genes within a species is genetic diversity, one component of biodiversity.

questions.stem: Evidence from fossils suggests that the number of species alive on Earth today, compared with the past, is:

options.text: Greater than at any time in the past
Lower than at any time in the past

questions.explanation: Fossil evidence suggests there are currently more species alive than at any time in the past.

questions.scenario: In classifying a group, 'splitters' recognise more species than 'lumpers'.
questions.stem: What does this illustrate?

options.text: The same observations can be classified in different ways
Classification is always identical for everyone

questions.explanation: Splitters and lumpers show that classification is pattern recognition and the same data can be grouped differently.

questions.stem: The current wave of extinctions is often called the:
questions.explanation: The current, largely anthropogenic extinctions constitute the sixth mass extinction.

questions.scenario: The North Island giant moa of New Zealand became extinct after human arrival.
questions.stem: This is used as a case study of the loss of:
questions.explanation: The giant moa is a case study of the loss of terrestrial megafauna due to human activity.

questions.scenario: The Caribbean monk seal has become extinct.
questions.stem: This is used as a case study of the loss of:
questions.explanation: The Caribbean monk seal is used as a case study of the extinction of a marine species.

questions.stem: The study of the sixth mass extinction focuses on causes that are:
questions.explanation: The syllabus focuses on the anthropogenic causes of the current mass extinction rather than non-anthropogenic causes of past ones.

questions.stem: A required case study of ecosystem loss is the loss of:

options.text: Mixed dipterocarp forest in Southeast Asia

questions.explanation: One required case study of ecosystem loss is the mixed dipterocarp forest of Southeast Asia.

questions.stem: The causes of ecosystem loss studied in this topic are those that are:
questions.explanation: Students study only causes of ecosystem loss that are directly or indirectly anthropogenic.

questions.stem: Which organisation's reports provide evidence for the biodiversity crisis?

options.text: IPBES (Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services)

questions.explanation: IPBES reports and similar sources provide evidence for the biodiversity crisis.

questions.scenario: To detect change in biodiversity, surveys of a habitat must be:
questions.stem: How should such surveys be carried out?
questions.explanation: Surveys must be repeated to provide evidence of change in species richness and evenness.

questions.stem: Biodiversity surveys often measure species richness and species:
questions.explanation: Surveys assess species richness (number of species) and evenness (relative abundance).

questions.stem: Which is described as an overarching cause of the current biodiversity crisis?

options.text: A decrease in the human population

questions.explanation: Human population growth is the overarching cause, driving the specific causes of biodiversity loss.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a specific cause of the current biodiversity crisis?

options.text: Deforestation and clearance of land for agriculture

questions.explanation: Specific causes include deforestation, over-exploitation, urbanization, pollution and invasive species.

questions.scenario: Non-native species are spread around the world by global transport.
questions.stem: These are referred to as:
questions.explanation: The spread of pests, diseases and invasive alien species through global transport is a cause of biodiversity loss.

questions.stem: Conserving species in their natural habitats is known as:
questions.explanation: In situ conservation protects species within their natural habitats, for example in nature reserves.

questions.scenario: Endangered animals are bred and kept in zoos.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Ex situ conservation protects species outside their natural habitat, such as in zoos and botanic gardens.

questions.scenario: Seeds of many plant species are stored in cold conditions for future use.
questions.stem: This conservation method uses:
questions.explanation: Storage of germ plasm in seed or tissue banks is a form of ex situ conservation.

questions.stem: Why is more than one approach to conservation needed?

options.text: No single approach is sufficient and species need different measures
One approach always works for every species

questions.explanation: No single approach is sufficient by itself, and different species require different conservation measures.

questions.stem: The EDGE of Existence programme prioritises species that are:
questions.explanation: EDGE stands for Evolutionarily Distinct and Globally Endangered species, which it prioritises for conservation.

questions.scenario: An 'evolutionarily distinct' species has few close relatives.
questions.stem: Why might such a species be prioritised for conservation?

options.text: Its loss would remove a unique branch of evolutionary history
It is easy to replace with a relative
It has many similar species already

questions.explanation: Evolutionarily distinct species represent unique evolutionary history that cannot be replaced if lost.

questions.scenario: Deciding which species to save involves ethical, political and economic factors.
questions.stem: This shows that conservation prioritisation:

options.text: Has complex implications that need to be debated

questions.explanation: Prioritisation has complex ethical, environmental, political, social, cultural and economic implications that must be debated.

questions.stem: For evidence to be verifiable, it usually needs to come from a source that has been:
questions.explanation: Verifiable evidence usually comes from peer-reviewed published sources that allow methodology to be checked.

questions.scenario: Members of the public help collect biodiversity data.
questions.stem: These contributors are known as:
questions.explanation: Citizen scientists contribute to biodiversity data collection, offering benefits but also methodological concerns.

questions.scenario: A region contains many different habitats such as forest, wetland and grassland.
questions.stem: This variety of habitats represents:
questions.explanation: The variety of different ecosystems in an area is ecosystem diversity, one component of biodiversity.

questions.stem: Which is an example of over-exploitation as a cause of biodiversity loss?

options.text: Hunting a species faster than it can reproduce

questions.explanation: Hunting and other over-exploitation remove individuals faster than populations can recover, reducing biodiversity.

questions.scenario: A degraded ecosystem is restored to its former natural state.
questions.stem: This conservation approach is called:

options.text: Ex situ conservation in a zoo

questions.explanation: Rewilding and reclamation of degraded ecosystems are in situ approaches to conservation.

questions.stem: Although millions of species have been described, scientists believe:

options.text: Many more species remain to be discovered
Every species has already been found

questions.explanation: Many species have been described, but there are many more still to be discovered.

questions.scenario: A forest is cleared and replaced with a city.
questions.stem: This cause of biodiversity loss is:
questions.explanation: Urbanization destroys natural habitat and is a specific cause of the current biodiversity crisis.

questions.stem: The number of different species present in a community is a measure of:
questions.explanation: Species diversity refers to the variety and number of species in a community.

questions.stem: A potential concern about data collected by citizen scientists is that it:

options.text: Is always more reliable than expert data

questions.explanation: Citizen-collected data brings benefits but also unique methodological concerns that must be considered.

questions.stem: Which of these is an in situ conservation method?

options.text: Storing seeds in a seed bank
Breeding plants in a botanic garden

questions.explanation: Managing nature reserves conserves species in their natural habitat (in situ); zoos, seed banks and botanic gardens are ex situ.

questions.stem: Which term describes extinction directly caused by human activity?
questions.explanation: Anthropogenic extinction is extinction caused by humans, the focus of the sixth mass extinction study.

questions.scenario: Chemicals released into a river harm the organisms living there.
questions.stem: This cause of biodiversity loss is:
questions.explanation: Pollution is one of the specific causes of the current biodiversity crisis.

questions.stem: Classifying organisms is described as an example of:
questions.explanation: Classification is an example of pattern recognition, and the same observations can be classified in different ways.

questions.scenario: Two endangered species compete for limited conservation funds; one is evolutionarily distinct.
questions.stem: Using EDGE reasoning, priority might be given to the:
questions.explanation: EDGE prioritises evolutionarily distinct and globally endangered species to preserve unique evolutionary history.

questions.stem: Loss of an entire habitat, such as a forest, is best described as:
questions.explanation: The destruction of a whole habitat is ecosystem loss, studied through anthropogenic case studies.

questions.stem: Which is required for reliable evidence of a biodiversity crisis?

options.text: Reliable surveys from a wide range of habitats
A single observation from one place

questions.explanation: Reliable surveys across a wide range of habitats, repeated over time, are needed as evidence.

questions.stem: Which set correctly lists specific causes of the current biodiversity crisis?

options.text: Over-exploitation, urbanization, deforestation, pollution, invasive species
Rewilding, seed banks, nature reserves, botanic gardens

questions.explanation: Specific causes include over-exploitation, urbanization, deforestation, pollution and invasive species, driven by population growth.

questions.stem: Biodiversity is best described as the variety of life at:

options.text: All its forms, levels and combinations

questions.explanation: Biodiversity is the variety of life in all its forms, levels and combinations, including ecosystems, species and genes.

questions.stem: Botanic gardens conserve plant species by:

options.text: Growing and maintaining them outside their natural habitat (ex situ)
Protecting them only in the wild

questions.explanation: Botanic gardens are an ex situ method, conserving plants outside their natural habitats.

questions.scenario: A student carries out a brief case study of a locally familiar extinct species.
questions.stem: This is encouraged in order to:

options.text: Give a range of causes of species extinction

questions.explanation: Three or more case studies, including a locally familiar one, give a range of causes of anthropogenic extinction.

questions.stem: How does human population growth act as an overarching cause of biodiversity loss?

options.text: It intensifies the specific causes such as deforestation and pollution
It has no impact on habitats

questions.explanation: Population growth increases demand for land and resources, intensifying the specific causes of biodiversity loss.

questions.scenario: A biodiversity survey is repeated in the same habitat ten years later.
questions.stem: The main purpose of repeating it is to:

options.text: Detect changes in species richness and evenness over time

questions.explanation: Repeating surveys provides evidence of change in species richness and evenness, showing whether biodiversity is declining.

questions.stem: Why might both in situ and ex situ methods be used together for one species?

options.text: Combining approaches gives a better chance of survival
Only one method is ever allowed
The methods cancel each other out

questions.explanation: Because no single approach is sufficient, combining in situ and ex situ methods improves conservation outcomes.

questions.stem: The 'GE' in the EDGE programme stands for:
questions.explanation: EDGE stands for Evolutionarily Distinct and Globally Endangered species.

questions.stem: The loss of mixed dipterocarp forest in Southeast Asia is mainly caused by:

options.text: Human activities such as logging and land clearance
An increase in the number of trees

questions.explanation: This required case study of ecosystem loss results from anthropogenic causes such as logging and clearance.

questions.stem: The difference between 'splitters' and 'lumpers' affects:

options.text: How many species are recognised in a group

questions.explanation: Splitters recognise more species than lumpers, showing that species counts depend on how observations are classified.

questions.stem: Which list contains only recognised conservation approaches?

options.text: Nature reserves, rewilding, zoos, seed banks

questions.explanation: Conservation approaches include nature reserves and rewilding (in situ) and zoos, botanic gardens and seed banks (ex situ).
